topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed across the screen, stark white against the dark backdrop of my laptop. *For Sale: 14 Willow Creek Lane.* My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary catch in my chest. It was impossible. After all these years, after the years I’d spent meticulously erasing every memory tied to that address, the listing was there. A realtor’s glossy photo of the porch swing, the exact shade of peeling blue paint on the shutters—it was all painfully familiar. I stared at the screen, my hands suddenly clammy, the keyboard feeling slick and alien beneath my fingertips. A slow, cold dread began to pool in my stomach, tightening with every passing second. I wanted to slam the laptop shut, to disappear into the shadows, but my feet remained rooted to the floor, paralyzed by the sheer, undeniable reality of what I was seeing.

[story 2]

"Are you sure you want to look at this, Leo?" Sarah asked, leaning over my shoulder, her voice laced with concern. I didn't look up. My eyes were glued to the property details popping up on the monitor. The address blinked mockingly: my childhood address. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim slightly. I felt a distinct tightening around my jaw, the muscles bunching involuntarily. "It's just an old listing, Sarah. Nothing," I managed to croak out, my voice sounding thin and reedy. But inside, a frantic alarm was ringing. Every nerve ending seemed to be screaming, a frantic buzzing beneath my skin. I could feel a flush creeping up my neck, hot and undeniable, as I tried desperately to appear composed while my entire internal structure felt dangerously fragile.

[story 3]

I reread the description three times, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. The address. It was the cornerstone of my carefully constructed present, the place I’d spent a decade building a life away from. Now, it was just a data point, an object of some stranger's desire. A profound sense of exposure washed over me, leaving a hollow, echoing emptiness in my chest. I pushed back from the desk so abruptly that my chair scraped loudly against the hardwood floor, the sudden noise jarring. I stood there, rigid, my gaze fixed on the screen, unable to look away, as if the listing held some kind of corrosive power. My palms started sweating again, a clammy film coating my skin, and I could feel the heat rising steadily across my cheeks, a deep, uncomfortable warmth that demanded attention.
